ontology.foodon_gap_note: FOODON_03301638 represents the carbonated beverage category; no single-entry exact code for generic carbonated soft drink exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226465004 represents carbonated drink as a substance category; used as closest parent for generic carbonated soft drink.

main_ingredients: food acids (e.g. citric acid or phosphoric acid)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard 350 ml bottle of carbonated soft drink, as commonly sold at Subway and similar fast-casual outlets in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Carbonated beverage, cola-type (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB No. 14400); supplemented with USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods generic carbonated soft drink values
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values represent a generic regular carbonated soft drink (cola-type, sugar-sweetened) per 100 g (approximately 100 ml), adapted from USDA SR Legacy NDB 14400 and USDA FoodData Central. This is a category-level estimate; actual product values vary by brand and flavour. Diet/zero-sugar variants would have near-zero energy and sugar.

gi_source.title: Coca-Cola, carbonated soft drink — GI value 63 (glucose reference); International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008, Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 63 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2008 International Tables (Atkinson et al.) for cola-type carbonated soft drink (Coca-Cola). This is used as the representative analogous estimate for a generic regular sugar-sweetened carbonated soft drink. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (63 × 37.1) / 100 ≈ 23.4; rounded to 24.9 using 10.6 g carb per 100 g × 3.5 serving factor (350 ml serving = 350 g × 10.6 / 100 = 37.1 g carb; GL = 63 × 37.1 / 100 = 23.4). Value documented as 24.9 is a minor rounding variant; corrected GL = 23.4 per standard formula.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (4 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Regular carbonated soft drinks are very low in sodium and do not directly raise blood pressure through sodium load, but high sugar intake from frequent consumption may contribute to weight gain and metabolic risk factors associated with hypertension; individuals with hypertension should limit intake to avoid excess caloric and sugar load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High free sugar content (10.6 g per 100 g; ~37 g per 350 ml serving) and medium glycemic index (GI 63)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sugar-sweetened carbonated soft drinks deliver a rapid glycemic load (GL ~23 per 350 ml serving) with no dietary fibre or protein to attenuate glucose absorption; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid regular sugar-sweetened carbonated drinks and consider diet or zero-sugar alternatives under clinical guidance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added sugar intake linked to dyslipidaemia and increased cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened carbonated beverages is associated with elevated triglycerides, reduced HDL cholesterol, and increased cardiovascular disease risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should minimise intake of sugar-sweetened carbonated drinks in line with WHO and Singapore HPB dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Carbonated soft drinks are sugar-sweetened, carbonated beverages produced by dissolving carbon dioxide gas under pressure into water combined with sugar, flavourings, food acids (such as citric acid or phosphoric acid), and preservatives. They are sold in bottles or cans and are consumed ready-to-drink. Common varieties include cola, lemon-lime, orange, and cream soda. In Singapore, carbonated soft drinks are widely available at fast-food outlets, convenience stores, and food delivery platforms such as field survey, where they are frequently paired with meals at outlets like Subway. A standard 350 ml serving of a regular sugar-sweetened carbonated soft drink provides approximately 41 kcal per 100 ml, with around 10.6 g of sugar per 100 ml and negligible protein, fat, and dietary fibre. The glycemic index of cola-type carbonated soft drinks is approximately 63 (medium GI), and a 350 ml serving delivers a glycemic load of approximately 23, making it a significant source of rapidly absorbed free sugars. From a clinical nutrition perspective, regular carbonated soft drinks are of high concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to their high free sugar content and medium-to-high glycemic load. They are also associated with increased cardiovascular risk through their contribution to excess caloric intake, weight gain, elevated triglycerides, and reduced HDL cholesterol. Sodium content is negligible, so direct hypertensive risk from sodium is low, though metabolic effects of excess sugar remain relevant. Diet and zero-sugar variants exist and have near-zero energy and glycemic impact. The food group is Beverages, subgroup carbonated soft drinks, and the processing level is ultra-processed.
common_user_queries: Is carbonated soft drink bad for diabetes?
How much sugar is in a bottle of soda?
Can I drink fizzy drinks if I have high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of cola?
Are carbonated drinks allowed on a diet?
How many calories in a 350ml bottle of soft drink?
What are the ingredients in carbonated drinks?
Is Sprite or Coke better for diabetics?
meal_context: beverage pairing with sandwiches and wraps

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 14400, cola-type carbonated soft drink), which is an approved secondary global source. The input record identifies the food only as 'Carbonated Bottled Drinks' without specifying brand or flavour; values represent a generic regular sugar-sweetened cola-type carbonated soft drink. Diet and zero-sugar variants would differ substantially. GI is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database via the 2008 International Tables (Atkinson et al.) for cola. Confidence is medium because the food identity is a generic category rather than a specific branded product.
notes: 1. protein_g is confirmed zero: carbonated soft drinks contain no protein by formulation; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 14400. 2. fat_g is confirmed zero: carbonated soft drinks contain no fat by formulation; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 3. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g are confirmed zero by product formulation. 4. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero: no dietary fibre present in carbonated soft drinks; confirmed zero per USDA source. 5. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero by formulation. 6. All vitamin values (vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1 through vitamin_b12, folate, vitamin_d, vitamin_e) are confirmed zero or effectively zero per USDA SR Legacy; carbonated soft drinks are not a source of vitamins. 7. Input record listed cuisine as 'Sandwich' and food_group as 'Vegetables'; both are incorrect for a carbonated beverage and have been corrected to 'Global / Western' and 'Beverages' respectively. 8. Glycemic load per serving recalculated: 350 ml serving = 350 g; carbohydrate per serving = 350 × 10.6 / 100 = 37.1 g; GL = 63 × 37.1 / 100 = 23.4. The gi_source notes document this calculation. The glycemic_load_per_serving field is set to 23.4. 9. FoodOn match type is closest_parent as no exact single-food FoodOn URI exists for generic carbonated soft drink. SNOMED CT match type is closest_parent. FoodEx2 AN0KL is an exact category match for soft drinks carbonated. 10. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the food is served in Singapore. 11. All -1 sentinel values have been replaced with confirmed zero (0.0) where USDA source explicitly reports zero or negligible amounts for carbonated soft drinks. No -1 values remain in per_100g as USDA SR Legacy provides full nutrient coverage for this food category.
